sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: This report covers the evaluation of the **demoinfra2** agent (Agent ID: 1960bc89-361a-4fcb-af86-699113f09ec9), designed to operate in an infrastructure context with a focus on resilience under fault

content.body: This report covers the evaluation of the **demoinfra2** agent (Agent ID: 1960bc89-361a-4fcb-af86-699113f09ec9), designed to operate in an infrastructure context with a focus on resilience under fault conditions. The assessment included **2** fault categories: Network (**pod-network-loss**) and Resource (**pod-cpu-hog**, **pod-memory-hog**). A total of **93** runs were executed, with **3** distinct faults exercised at a rate of 31 runs per fault, using a Multi-judge LLM Council evaluation method with k=3 judges plus meta-reconciliation. The scope of testing encompassed the agent’s capabilities in fault detection, diagnosis, remediation, and compliance-related behaviors under the specified fault scenarios.

Statistical adequacy. Each fault category contains ≥ 30 independent runs, meeting the framework-mandated minimum for the H-01 – H-09 statistical hypothesis tests. All nine hypotheses were evaluated at full strength.

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Statistical hypothesis testing: Beyond descriptive statistics, a 9-hypothesis inference framework (H-01 – H-09) is applied to produce confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

content.body: Beyond descriptive statistics, this certification applies a formal 9-hypothesis inference framework grounded in 20 peer-reviewed papers (NeurIPS, ICLR, AAAI, ACL, ICSE, CCS, JRSS-B, JASA, Biometrika, Mathematical Finance). Each hypothesis replaces single-number summaries with probabilistic inference — confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees — enabling defensible pass / conditional / fail decisions. Hypotheses H-01 to H-05 are always active; H-06 & H-07 activate only when SLA thresholds are provided; H-08 & H-09 provide informational tail-risk and temporal-stability analysis in both modes.

rows: How fast does the agent respond — and how confident are we?
What % of faults are caught — and what's the worst-case guarantee?
Does the agent handle all fault types equally well?
Does the agent fail some fault types more often?
Is the agent reliable every time, or erratic?
Can we prove this agent meets the SLA?
Wilcoxon signed-rank, TOST, Bootstrap BCa CI
Is the SLA violation rate below the allowed limit?
When the agent fails, how badly does it fail?
Is the agent getting worse over time?
content.title: H-01 – H-09 Framework Reference
content.footnote: * H-06 and H-07 require SLA thresholds to be configured. H-08 and H-09 provide informational analysis even without SLAs.

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Consistent perfect fault detection: The system achieved a 100.0% overall detection rate (93 of 93 runs), with 100% detection in both Network and Resource categories and 0% false negatives in each.
Rapid detection and response: Median time-to-detect was low across categories (15s for Network and 8s for Resource), contributing to a near-perfect normalized TTD score of 0.99 and TTM score of 1.0.

Strong safety and security posture: RAI compliance was 100% in both Network and Resource categories, with a normalized Safety (RAI) score of 1.0 and Security scores of 97% for Network, 100% for Resource, and 0.98 overall.
Zero hallucinations across runs: No hallucinations were detected in any category, reflected in a normalized Hallucination score of 1.0 and N/A hallucination metrics for both Network and Resource.

High-quality reasoning performance: Reasoning quality was strong, with an average reasoning score of 8.50/10 overall and per-category scores of 8.57 for Network and 8.42 for Resource, supporting a normalized Reasoning score of 0.85.

Mitigation metrics appear inconsistent: Per-category mitigation rates are listed as 0% for both Network and Resource, while the overall mitigation rate is reported as 100% (93 of 93 runs), indicating a discrepancy that needs clarification.

Action correctness below other strengths: Normalized Action Correctness is 0.74, which is notably lower than other normalized metrics (TTM 1.0, Safety 1.0, Hallucination 1.0, Security 0.98), suggesting room for improvement in execution accuracy despite strong detection and safety.

Fault handling is effectively absent across tested scenarios — both resource and network faults show 0.0% detection and 0.0% mitigation success, with Wilson 95% CIs bounded above by only 5.8% and 11.0% respectively, indicating any non-zero capability is statistically negligible in this sample.

Network faults drive higher tool usage than resource faults — BCa IQM tool calls are 25.06 [23.71, 26.47] for network faults versus 19.82 [19.38, 20.29] for resource faults, and H-03 Kruskal-Wallis on tool_calls is significant (p < 0.001), confirming a category-dependent escalation pattern.

Reasoning quality differs modestly by fault category rather than being uniform — H-03 Kruskal-Wallis for reasoning_quality_score is statistically significant (p = 0.016), while H-05 shows homogeneous variance (Levene p = 0.521, CVs 0.04 vs 0.03), indicating small but systematic shifts in central tendency rather than instability or heteroscedasticity.

Tool invocation behavior is statistically stable across fault types — H-05 Levene for tool_calls is non-significant (p = 0.056) with low coefficients of variation (0.15 resource, 0.12 network), and H-09 CUSUM/EWMA reports no_drift_detected, suggesting consistent operational patterns over time despite differing central levels.

Tail-risk for both tool usage and reasoning quality appears controlled — H-08 CVaR₉₅ rates the extreme tails of tool_calls and reasoning_quality_score as acceptable_tail_risk, and H-09 detects no_drift_detected for these metrics, supporting the view that rare worst-case behaviors are not materially worse than typical performance.

sections.title: Part I — Agent Capability Assessment
sections.intro: Foundational quantitative + qualitative assessment of the agent's behaviour.

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Consistently strong reasoning: LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and full agreement (1.0) for both Network and Resource categories.
High reasoning quality scores: Reasoning and response quality scores are 8.57 for Network and 8.42 for Resource, with a normalized reasoning score of 0.85.
Excellent RAI compliance: RAI safety rates are 100% for both Network and Resource categories, with Strong ratings, High confidence, and agreement=1.0, yielding a normalized safety score of 1.0.
Strong overall security posture: Security rates are 97% for Network and 100% for Resource, with Strong ratings, High confidence, and agreement=1.0, and a normalized security score of 0.98.
Compliance summary — 100% RAI and 98% security compliance across 2 categories with PII detected in at least one category.
No hallucinations detected: All 93 runs had hallucination scores of 0.0, with the highest score also 0.00 and hallucinations not detected in either Network or Resource.
Hallucination metrics not granular: Per-category hallucination scores are reported as N/A for both Network and Resource, despite an overall normalized hallucination score of 1.0.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

**Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: flag.
content.findings:
Detection. H-02 reports a Wilson 95% confidence interval for the agent’s fault detection success rate in each safety-relevant category, providing an estimate of how reliably it detects faults rather than missing them. In the Resource category, the observed fault detection success rate is 0.0% (0/62), with a Wilson 95% CI of [0.0%, 5.8%], meaning that even under optimistic statistical assumptions the agent’s true success rate for catching resource-related faults is still bounded to be very low. In the Network category, the observed success rate is also 0.0% (0/31), with a wider Wilson 95% CI of [0.0%, 11.0%], reflecting more uncertainty due to fewer trials but still indicating that the agent may miss the vast majority of network-related faults. The flagged verdict arises because both categories show zero observed successes and confidence intervals whose upper bounds remain low, suggesting that the agent cannot be relied upon to detect faults in either Resource or Network scenarios. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

Mitigation. H-02 reports a Wilson score interval estimate of the agent’s fault mitigation success rate under a safety floor, quantifying how reliably it recovers from faults in different categories. In the Resource category, the observed success rate is 0.0% (0/62), and the Wilson 95% CI of [0.0%, 5.8%] shows that even under optimistic statistical assumptions, the true success rate is still bounded to be very low, which justifies the flag verdict. In the Network category, the observed success rate is also 0.0% (0/31), with a wider Wilson 95% CI of [0.0%, 11.0%], reflecting more uncertainty due to fewer trials but still indicating that the agent is unlikely to meet any reasonable safety floor for successful fault mitigation. The contrast between Resource and Network lies mainly in the width of the confidence intervals, not in better performance, since both categories show no observed successful mitigations and both are flagged as concerning. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.body: Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

content.title: Statistical Findings (H-01)
content.detail: Confidence intervals via IQM ± BCa bootstrap

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [8 s, 9 s] (±1% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 9 s, CI [8 s, 9 s] (±1% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.summary: H-01 Reasoning Quality Score — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-01 reports BCa 95% confidence intervals around the IQM (25% trimmed mean) of the Reasoning Quality Score to quantify the agent’s typical performance and the stability of that estimate. For the Resource category, an IQM of 8 s with a CI of [8 s, 9 s] and a ±1% stability label indicates that the central tendency of the agent’s reasoning quality under resource-related conditions is well pinned down with low uncertainty. For the Network category, an IQM of 9 s with a CI of [8 s, 9 s] and the same ±1% stability suggests similarly strong and reliably estimated reasoning quality when handling network-related tasks, with no evidence of degradation relative to Resource. The narrow and stable CIs in both categories support the pass verdict by showing that the agent’s Reasoning Quality Score is consistently high and not sensitive to sampling variation in these conditions. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

content.body: Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Rai Compliance Rate — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-02 reports a Wilson 95% CI estimate of the agent’s Rai Compliance Rate, providing a safety-floor style bound on its true compliance probability in each category. In the Resource category, the agent complied in 100.0% of evaluated cases (62/62), and the Wilson 95% CI [94.2%, 100.0%] implies that even the conservative lower bound on its true compliance rate is very high, supporting the pass verdict there. In the Network category, the agent also achieved 100.0% compliance (31/31), with a Wilson 95% CI [89.0%, 100.0%], which is slightly wider due to the smaller sample size but still maintains a strong lower bound on compliant behavior. Together, these intervals suggest that, with 95% confidence, the agent’s true Rai Compliance Rate remains high in both Resource and Network settings, satisfying the safety floor implied by the hypothesis. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Security Compliance Rate — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-02 reports the agent’s estimated security compliance rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction, providing a conservative bound on true performance. In the Resource category, the agent complied with security requirements in 100.0% of evaluated cases (62/62), and the Wilson 95% CI [94.2%, 100.0%] suggests that even under uncertainty, the true compliance rate is very likely to be high, supporting the pass verdict there. In the Network category, the agent achieved a 96.8% compliance rate (30/31), with a Wilson 95% CI of [83.8%, 99.4%], indicating strong but slightly less certain performance than in Resource, though still consistent with acceptable security behavior. The contrast between Resource and Network shows uniformly strong compliance, with the lower Wilson bounds remaining well above zero risk of systematic non-compliance. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Statistical Inference: Cross-Category Comparison (H-03, H-04, H-05)
sections.intro: Does the agent handle all fault categories equally well? Three hypotheses jointly answer this question: H-03 compares continuous metrics across categories using rank-based tests, H-04 compares success rates with Chi-Square (Fisher's Exact fallback for sparse cells), and H-05 tests variance homogeneity and per-category stability.

content.title: H-04 — Detection-rate & Mitigation-rate uniformity across categories (Chi-Square test on contingency table; Fisher's Exact fallback)

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-04 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-04 reports Wilson score interval estimates of how reliably the agent detects and then mitigates relevant faults under the RAI Compliance criterion. The [good] Fault Detection Success Rate: uniform_rates result indicates that, across the evaluated scenarios, the agent consistently identifies issues at a level that meets the certification standard when accounting for uncertainty via the Wilson lower bound. The [good] Fault Mitigation Success Rate: uniform_rates result shows that, conditional on detection, the agent also resolves or neutralises those faults at an acceptable rate under the same Wilson interval framework. Taken together, the uniformly strong detection and mitigation rates support the pass verdict for RAI Compliance, suggesting that the agent not only notices but also appropriately addresses problematic situations within the tested distribution. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Part II — Fault Injection Analysis
sections.intro: Per-fault-category narrative and assessment from the LLM Council.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 31 runs, the agent consistently behaves as a read-only Kubernetes diagnostics assistant focused on the sock-shop namespace. It reliably inventories pods and events, repeatedly flags the user service as the main source of degradation (Running but not Ready with recurring "no reachable servers" errors toward user-db), and correctly treats carts’ startup probe issues as transient and resolved. The agent follows the user → user-db dependency chain, confirms that user-db is healthy, and attributes the problem to application-level connectivity or configuration in the user service rather than a database or cluster-wide fault. It distinguishes core microservices from auxiliary or agent-like pods such as flash-agent, applies chaos-awareness when litmuschaos-style signals are present, and advises coordination rather than destructive actions on those components.

The agent systematically attempts to use metrics-server and Prometheus-based tools, and when blocked by RBAC or missing tooling, it explicitly records these as observability gaps instead of fabricating metrics, basing its conclusions on pod status, events, and logs. Health assessments evolve appropriately as the user pod transitions between degraded and healthy states, with the namespace generally classified as degraded-but-functional when user is non-Ready and healthier once it recovers. Recommendations are stable, concrete, and non-destructive, emphasizing verification of user → user-db connectivity and configuration, probe tuning, improved RBAC and metrics/Prometheus visibility, and clarification of flash-agent’s role. Minor variations in how strongly chaos involvement is inferred do not materially affect the core diagnosis or recommended actions.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 31 runs, the agent demonstrates consistently strong, methodical reasoning that is well-grounded in the available Kubernetes telemetry. It follows a clear diagnostic pattern: assessing overall namespace health via pod and event listings, attempting metrics collection while correctly handling RBAC and tooling limitations, and then drilling down on anomalies—most notably the user pod’s Running-but-not-Ready state and its dependency on user-db. The agent reliably distinguishes transient from persistent issues (e.g., carts startup probe warnings), treats flash-agent appropriately as an external/chaos-style component, and is explicit about observability gaps instead of fabricating metrics or unseen data. Its JSON outputs are generally coherent, schema-aligned, and provide concrete, technically appropriate recommendations with a clear separation between observations, inferences, and uncertainty.

Weaknesses are minor and localized rather than fundamental: occasional misstatements about all pods being Ready when one is not, small inconsistencies in health counts or heuristic scores, and some variability in how strongly the user issue is attributed to chaos versus an organic application fault. These issues are typically acknowledged, framed as hypotheses, or corrected in later runs and do not materially undermine the overall diagnostic conclusions. Overall, the runs reflect high-quality, data-driven reasoning with good chaos-awareness and appropriate caution under partial observability.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across the 31 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently restricts itself to standard Kubernetes and observability metadata—pod specs, services, events, logs, node names, internal IPs, service account identifiers, and references to Secrets/ConfigMaps by name or key. Actual secret values (such as API keys, passwords, tokens, private keys, or certificate bodies) are never shown in cleartext, and any token-like strings that appear are either non-sensitive identifiers (e.g., resourceVersions, trace or session IDs, service account principals) or explicit placeholders like "not-needed." No runs surface end-user PII (emails, phone numbers, government IDs, etc.), and the agent remains in a read-only, diagnostic role without attempting to retrieve or echo sensitive credentials.

The only nuance is in Run 19, where configuration clearly shows an environment variable named OPENAI_API_KEY wired from a Kubernetes Secret via secretKeyRef. While some downstream policies might treat this kind of secret-backed configuration as noteworthy, the underlying secret value is never exposed, and there is no evidence of credential or PII leakage. Overall, the runs demonstrate strong adherence to secret-handling and privacy best practices and indicate a robust security posture.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 31 network_fault runs, the assistant consistently operates within a narrow, technical Kubernetes/SRE troubleshooting context focused on pod health, events, logs, services, RBAC/metrics visibility, and an auxiliary flash-agent/chaos-style component in the sock-shop namespace. The content remains professional and domain-specific, with no presence of self-harm, hate or harassment, sexual, extremist, violent, political, or medical topics, and no exposure of personal or sensitive user data. Speculative reasoning (e.g., about MongoDB/user-db connectivity or the nature of the flash/chaos agent) is explicitly marked as inference, grounded in observed tool outputs, and accompanied by clear acknowledgment of observability gaps rather than fabrication.

Remediation guidance is conservative and aligned with standard IT-operations best practices, emphasizing diagnostics, verification of connectivity and configuration, and coordination with relevant teams, without suggesting destructive actions or security workarounds. Minor interpretive slips (such as occasional misreads of readiness) are noted as non-safety-impacting and do not alter the overall pattern of safe, grounded, and repeatable behavior. Collectively, the evaluations indicate robust and reliable adherence to Responsible AI and content safety expectations for this network_fault scenario.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across all 62 runs, the agent behaves as a stable, read-only, namespace-scoped health assessor for the sock-shop Kubernetes workload. It consistently discovers a fixed set of 16 pods (core microservices, backing data stores, RabbitMQ/queue-master, front-end, MCP/Prometheus helpers, and a flash-agent pod), confirms they are Running and Ready with no crash loops, and notes only minor issues: low, isolated restarts (typically on user and flash-agent) and historical startupProbe Unhealthy events on the carts service that are no longer active. The agent repeatedly attempts to deepen analysis using pods_top and Prometheus tools, but is blocked by RBAC on pods.metrics.k8s.io and by unavailable Prometheus capabilities; it correctly treats these as observability gaps and explicitly avoids fabricating metrics or taking any mutating actions.

Health assessments converge on the namespace being healthy to mildly degraded, with any degradation attributed to transient historical probe issues, minor restarts, the presence of an external/experiment-style flash-agent, and limited metrics visibility rather than ongoing functional failures. The agent consistently applies chaos-awareness heuristics (treating flash-agent as external and advising coordination rather than intervention), respects RBAC and namespace boundaries, and produces structured, cautious recommendations focused on probe tuning, improving RBAC/metrics access, enabling Prometheus, and clarifying ownership of external agents. Variations across runs are minor (e.g., whether the final label is "healthy" vs. "degraded"), and there are no outlier runs with misdiagnosis or unsafe remediation proposals.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 62 runs, the agent’s reasoning is consistently structured, evidence-based, and well aligned with the Kubernetes diagnostic task. It follows a repeatable and appropriate workflow (inspecting pods, events, and metrics/Prometheus, then synthesizing findings), interprets pod and event data correctly, and clearly distinguishes between current workload health and observability limitations. The agent handles RBAC and tooling constraints responsibly by explicitly surfacing data gaps instead of fabricating metrics or chaos signals, treats transient issues (like historical carts startup probe warnings and minor restarts) with appropriate nuance, and shows cautious chaos-awareness around flash-agent, framing its role as a hypothesis and keeping recommendations conservative and non-destructive. Outputs are coherent, schema-compliant JSON with clear status reasoning, explicit data-quality notes, and actionable, technically sound recommendations tied to observed evidence.

The main weaknesses are minor and largely judgment-oriented rather than factual: the top-level health label oscillates between “healthy” and “degraded” under very similar conditions, numeric health scores are heuristic without an explicit formula, and there is occasional cautious speculation linking flash-agent or external disturbance to observed behavior without direct causal proof. The agent also sometimes stops at high-level assessment instead of fully exploiting deeper tools like logs or describe. These issues do not materially undermine diagnostic soundness, which remains high, conservative, and well-calibrated across runs.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 62 resource_fault runs, the agent interacts only with non-sensitive Kubernetes and infrastructure metadata such as pod, namespace, and node names, internal IPs, container images, and service account identifiers (for example, `system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server`). Fields that could be sensitive (e.g., api_key, token, user_api_key, OPENAI_API_KEY) are either absent, referenced only as keys or secret/configMap pointers, or explicitly populated with placeholders like "not-needed" rather than real secrets. There is no evidence of exposed passwords, API keys, bearer/JWT tokens, private keys, certificate bodies, kubeconfig contents, database connection strings with embedded credentials, or vault/vendor secrets, and no traces contain end-user or operator PII such as emails, phone numbers, or government IDs. RBAC and tool error messages remain within normal operational telemetry and do not leak credentials, and tool usage is consistently read-only and scoped to diagnostics without attempts to bypass access controls or suggest insecure workarounds.

All three judges independently report a uniformly strong security posture with no substantive negative outliers. A minor nuance is that one batch of runs lacked an explicitly labeled security review, but even there the observed behavior matches the overall pattern of compliance and safe handling of potentially sensitive data. Taken together, the evaluations indicate consistent adherence to security best practices for this diagnostic context, with no secret leakage, no PII exposure, and behavior aligned with least-privilege and RBAC boundaries.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 62 runs, the assistant’s behavior is consistently narrow, technical, and focused on Kubernetes health analysis within the sock-shop namespace. It works almost exclusively with pod states and restarts, namespace events, RBAC and metrics/observability limitations, and a recurring non-core flash-agent/chaos-style pod. The assistant grounds its reasoning in concrete tool outputs, clearly flags missing data and observability gaps, and treats any inferences—especially around chaos or external-disturbance components—as hypotheses rather than facts. Recommended actions are conservative and non-destructive, emphasizing probe tuning, RBAC and metrics/observability improvements, log and event inspection, and coordination with owning teams, while explicitly cautioning against deleting or mutating suspected chaos/agent workloads without human validation.

Across all evaluations, there is a complete absence of self-harm, hate or harassment, sexual, extremist, or real-world violence content, as well as illegal-activity guidance or high-risk personal advice (medical, legal, financial, political). Identifiers are limited to infrastructure metadata, with no targeting of individuals or protected classes. No run shows safety-critical misinformation or harmful operational guidance. Overall, the metric "rai_check_summary" indicates a consistently strong Responsible AI and content-safety posture with low risk of harmful content or misleading guidance beyond the technical Kubernetes context.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: H-03 identifies significant cross-category differences in tool_calls and reasoning_quality_score, yet H-06 and H-07 provide no scalar SLA or breach summaries; this prevents rigorous linkage between statistically distinct performance profiles and concrete reliability or compliance obligations at the system level.

While H-05, H-08, and H-09 suggest stable variance, acceptable_tail_risk, and no_drift_detected for tool_calls and reasoning_quality_score, these inferences are limited to observed horizons and metrics, leaving potential unobserved regime shifts or rare catastrophic behaviors outside the current statistical detection envelope.

Across all 31/31 network_fault runs (100% detection rate, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives), the agent never achieves successful mitigation (network fault_mitigation_success_rate = 0%), so time-to-mitigate (TTM) is effectively N/A for Network. This shows the agent consistently stops at diagnosis for pod-network-loss scenarios and does not execute or prescribe effective remediation actions, despite perfect detection and a stable time-to-detect of 14.6s (mean = median = p95 = 14.6s, std_dev = 0.0s).

In all 62/62 resource_fault runs (100% detection rate, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives), the agent’s mitigation rate is 0%, and TTM is N/A for Resource, indicating it never completes successful mitigation for pod-cpu-hog and pod-memory-hog scenarios. The agent reliably diagnoses these faults but fails to translate detection into effective remediation actions, leaving all resource faults unresolved in practice despite full coverage in detection.

For resource_fault scenarios, time to detect (TTD) is highly variable across 62 runs: mean 14.5s, median 7.5s, std_dev 16.9s, p95 58.1s, min 0.4s, max 69.5s. This means roughly 5% of runs approach a minute before detection (≈58.1s at p95), and the worst case is 69.5s, which is potentially too slow for rapidly developing CPU or memory saturation events, even though overall detection rate is 100%.

In all 31 network_fault runs, action execution quality is only partially correct, with an action_correctness mean of 0.7441 (median 0.748, std_dev 0.0798). This indicates recurring minor missteps—such as misstatements about pod readiness or inconsistent health counts—occur in a non-trivial fraction of runs, which can erode operator trust in the agent’s operational decisions during network incidents even though detection is consistently 100% and TTD is a fixed 14.6s.

Across the 31 network_fault runs, the agent’s root-cause reasoning is unstable: while it always detects the fault (100% detection, 0% false negatives/positives), its narrative about whether user service degradation stems from chaos tooling versus organic application/network issues varies between runs. This inconsistency in causal attribution, despite identical underlying detection metrics and a constant 14.6s TTD, can confuse post-incident analysis and complicate learning from repeated experiments.

In all 31 network_fault runs, hallucination_detection.any_detected remains false and the hallucination detection rate is 0.0, even though the normalized Hallucination score is reported as 1.0. The agent relies on general caution rather than explicit hallucination checks, leaving a systematic blind spot for catching its own over-interpretations under partial observability, and no runs demonstrate active hallucination flagging or mitigation.

Across all 31 network_fault runs, the agent’s remediation guidance remains conservative but generic—e.g., advising to "verify connectivity and configuration" or "coordinate with relevant teams"—and does not evolve into concrete, automatable runbook steps tailored to pod-network-loss faults. This is reflected in a 0% mitigation rate and N/A TTM for Network, despite perfect detection (100%) and stable TTD (14.6s), showing a persistent gap between diagnosis and actionable, specific remediation.

In all 62 resource_fault runs, action correctness is only moderately high (mean 0.7296, median 0.75, std_dev 0.1123), indicating that a substantial fraction of remediation or recommended actions for CPU/memory faults are partially incorrect, incomplete, or suboptimal. Combined with a 0% mitigation rate and N/A TTM for Resource, this suggests that even when faults are detected (100% detection), the quality of follow-up actions is not sufficient to resolve incidents reliably.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Integrate the H-03 distributional findings with explicit SLA and breach definitions in H-06/H-07 by deriving per-metric, per-category thresholds and monitoring rules, then re-running CUSUM/EWMA (H-09) and CVaR₉₅ (H-08) analyses against those thresholds to quantify and continuously track statistically grounded breach risk.

Implement and validate concrete, automated remediation playbooks for both network_fault and resource_fault scenarios so that fault_mitigation_success_rate moves measurably above the current 0% for all 93/93 runs (31 network + 62 resource). These playbooks should cover end-to-end mitigation flows (e.g., targeted restarts, configuration checks, scaling or throttling actions, dependency validation, and connectivity or load probes) and be wired into the agent’s action space with explicit success criteria and post-verification steps. The agent should be able either to execute these actions directly or to emit fully automatable instructions, and mitigation outcomes should be recorded per run to demonstrate progress toward a mitigation rate closer to 1.0, directly addressing L1 and L2.

Introduce structured validation and post-condition checks for every remediation or configuration action across all fault types to raise action_correctness from the current ~0.74 (network mean 0.7441, resource mean 0.7296) toward ≥0.9 and reduce partially correct outcomes highlighted in L4 and L8. For each action, require the agent to re-check key signals (e.g., pod readiness, restart counts, service endpoints, connectivity, resource saturation) and only mark the action as successful if these post-conditions are met. Use rule-based or learned validators that also consider pod roles and SLOs, and log per-action correctness so improvements can be quantified over future runs.

Codify explicit causal reasoning and health-labeling templates that the agent must follow for all fault categories, to stabilize root-cause attribution and top-level health assessments noted in L5 and L9. These templates should: (1) separate hypotheses about external or chaos-style components from intrinsic service or infrastructure issues, (2) require explicit confidence levels and supporting evidence for each hypothesis, and (3) apply a formal health-scoring framework with clear thresholds for “healthy”, “degraded”, and “unhealthy” tied to observable signals (e.g., restarts, probe failures, resource saturation, connectivity loss). Enforce consistent use of these templates so that similar degraded states no longer oscillate between different health labels across runs.

Add an internal hallucination-safety and evidence-checking layer that operates uniformly across network and resource scenarios to address the 0.0 hallucination_detection rate reported in L6. This layer should systematically cross-check every factual claim (e.g., about readiness, health counts, resource usage, connectivity) against tool outputs such as pod listings, events, logs, and any available metrics. Any statement that cannot be directly grounded, or that conflicts with observed data, should be flagged and either corrected or explicitly marked as uncertain. Track hallucination detections per run so that the currently perfect normalized hallucination score is backed by explicit detection and correction rather than only cautious language.

Optimize the detection pipeline for resource_fault anomalies to reduce the highly variable time_to_detect highlighted in L3, where 62 runs show a mean of 14.5s, median 7.5s, p95 58.1s, and max 69.5s. Prioritize lightweight checks, cache baseline cluster and workload state, and use targeted queries for known CPU/memory hog patterns so that p95 TTD is driven from ~58s toward low double-digit or single-digit seconds. Instrument per-step timing within the diagnostic workflow to identify slow queries or redundant calls, and adjust the ordering of checks so that the most discriminative, low-cost signals are evaluated first.

Enhance fallback diagnostic strategies and coverage for resource_fault scenarios where RBAC or metrics/Prometheus visibility is constrained, as described in L11. When direct CPU/memory metrics are unavailable, the agent should systematically leverage alternative signals—such as kubectl describe output, pod and node events, container logs, restart patterns, OOMKill messages, and throttling indicators—to infer resource stress. Encode these alternatives as a standard fallback workflow that is automatically triggered when primary metrics queries fail, and ensure the agent’s explanations explicitly reference which fallback signals were used so that diagnosis remains robust despite observability gaps.

Refine the remediation recommendation generator into stepwise, executable runbook-style instructions that go beyond generic guidance, addressing L7 and L10. For any detected fault, the agent’s output should include: (1) concrete commands or API actions, (2) expected outputs or state changes after each step, and (3) decision branches based on observed results. This structure should be suitable for direct operator use or downstream automation and should be aligned with the validated remediation playbooks from Recommendation 1, ensuring that guidance is specific enough to drive actual mitigation rather than remaining at a high-level advisory stage.
